title: Personal Loan Interest Rates Explained | CreditDoc
h1: Personal Loan Interest Rates Explained (What Actually Drives Your Rate)
meta_description: Learn what drives personal loan interest rates, how credit union rates compare, whether rates are dropping, and how to lock in the lowest APR you qualify for.
questions_answered: what are credit union personal loan rates
are personal loan interest rates going down
are personal loans interest rates low
can i change my personal loan interest rate
do personal loan interest rates change
do personal loans have higher interest rates
does personal loan interest rate change
does personal loan interest rate change with repo rate

sections.heading: What Determines Your Personal Loan Interest Rate
sections.content:
Your personal loan interest rate isn't a single number pulled from thin air. Lenders weigh several factors, and understanding them gives you real leverage when shopping.

**Credit score** is the biggest driver. According to the Federal Reserve's data on consumer credit, borrowers with excellent credit (typically 720+) receive rates dramatically lower than those with fair or poor scores. Here's a general breakdown of how credit tiers affect personal loan APRs:

| Credit Tier | FICO Score Range | Typical APR Range |
|---|---|---|
| Excellent | 720–850 | 7%–12% |
| Good | 690–719 | 12%–18% |
| Fair | 630–689 | 18%–25% |
| Poor | 300–629 | 25%–36% |

Beyond your credit score, lenders also consider your debt-to-income ratio, employment history, loan amount, and repayment term. A shorter term usually means a lower rate because the lender's money is at risk for less time.

One thing many borrowers miss: the difference between a rate and an APR. The APR includes origination fees and other costs, so it's the true cost of borrowing. Always compare APRs, not just interest rates, when evaluating offers.

sections.heading: What Are Credit Union Personal Loan Rates
sections.content:
Credit unions consistently offer some of the lowest personal loan rates available. Because they're member-owned nonprofits, they don't need to maximize profit the way banks and online lenders do.

According to the National Credit Union Administration (NCUA), the average interest rate on a 36-month unsecured personal loan from a federal credit union has historically run 1 to 3 percentage points lower than comparable bank products. The NCUA publishes quarterly data on credit union rates, and the gap has remained steady over multiple rate cycles.

### Why Credit Unions Charge Less

- **Nonprofit structure.** Earnings go back to members through lower rates and fewer fees.
- **Relationship lending.** Credit unions may weigh your full financial picture, not just a FICO number.
- **Lower overhead.** Many credit unions operate with leaner cost structures than national banks.

### The Catch

You have to qualify for membership. Most credit unions require you to live in a certain area, work for a specific employer, or belong to a particular group. Some have broad eligibility through affiliated organizations. If you're not already a member, it's worth checking your local credit union's requirements — the rate savings can easily justify the effort.

For borrowers with less-than-perfect credit, credit unions are especially worth exploring. Some offer credit builder loans specifically designed to help you establish or rebuild your credit history while borrowing at reasonable rates.

sections.heading: Are Personal Loan Interest Rates Going Down
sections.content:
Whether personal loan rates are rising or falling depends almost entirely on the Federal Reserve's monetary policy. The Fed doesn't set personal loan rates directly, but it controls the federal funds rate, which influences what lenders pay to borrow money — and those costs get passed to you.

Here's how the chain works:

1. The Federal Reserve raises or lowers the federal funds rate.
2. Banks and lenders adjust their prime rate accordingly.
3. Personal loan rates shift in response, though not always immediately or by the same amount.

The Federal Reserve publishes its rate decisions and economic projections after each Federal Open Market Committee meeting. When the Fed signals rate cuts, personal loan rates tend to drift lower over the following months. When the Fed tightens, rates climb.

### What About the Repo Rate

Some borrowers ask whether the repo rate (repurchase agreement rate) affects personal loans. The repo rate is a short-term benchmark used in interbank lending. While it influences overall liquidity in the financial system, its effect on consumer personal loans is indirect. The federal funds rate is a much more direct driver of the rates you'll see quoted by personal loan lenders.

The bottom line: if you're waiting for rates to drop before borrowing, watch the Fed's forward guidance rather than trying to time the market perfectly. Rate changes take weeks or months to filter through to consumer products.

sections.heading: Do Personal Loans Have Higher Interest Rates Than Other Loans
sections.content:
Yes, personal loans generally carry higher interest rates than secured forms of borrowing. That's because most personal loans are unsecured — the lender has no collateral to seize if you stop paying.

Here's how personal loan rates typically compare:

| Loan Type | Secured? | Typical APR Range |
|---|---|---|
| Mortgage | Yes (home) | 6%–8% |
| Auto loan | Yes (vehicle) | 5%–10% |
| Home equity loan | Yes (home) | 7%–10% |
| Personal loan | Usually no | 7%–36% |
| Credit card | No | 18%–30% |

The wide range on personal loans reflects the credit-tier spread. A borrower with excellent credit might get a personal loan rate that rivals a home equity loan. A borrower with poor credit could pay rates approaching credit card territory.

That said, personal loans are almost always cheaper than credit cards for the same borrower. If you're carrying high-interest credit card debt, using a personal loan for debt consolidation can save you hundreds or thousands in interest — especially with a fixed rate that won't creep up over time.

The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau recommends comparing at least three to five offers before committing to any loan. Many lenders now offer prequalification with a soft inquiry, which means checking rates won't ding your credit score.

sections.heading: Fixed vs. Variable Rates: Does Your Personal Loan Rate Change
sections.content:
Most personal loans come with a fixed interest rate. That means the rate you're quoted at closing is the rate you'll pay for the entire life of the loan. Your monthly payment stays the same from the first month to the last.

Some lenders do offer variable-rate personal loans, though they're less common. With a variable rate:

- Your rate is tied to a benchmark index (usually the prime rate or SOFR).
- When the benchmark moves, your rate adjusts — sometimes quarterly, sometimes monthly.
- Your monthly payment can increase or decrease over the loan term.

### Which Should You Choose

**Fixed rate** makes sense when you want predictable payments and you're borrowing during a period of relatively low rates. You lock in your cost and budget with confidence.

**Variable rate** might save you money if rates are expected to drop, but you're taking on the risk that they could rise instead. For most borrowers taking out a personal loan for a specific purpose — paying off debt, funding a project, covering an expense — the certainty of a fixed rate is worth more than the potential savings of a variable one.

If you already have a personal loan and the rate environment has shifted significantly, refinancing into a new loan at a lower rate is an option. You can't typically call your lender and negotiate a lower rate on an existing fixed-rate personal loan the way you might with a credit card. You'd need to apply for an entirely new loan and use it to pay off the old one.

sections.heading: Can You Change or Lower Your Personal Loan Interest Rate
sections.content:
You can't edit your existing fixed-rate personal loan like updating a subscription plan. But you have two practical paths to a lower rate:

### 1. Refinance Your Personal Loan

Refinancing means taking out a new loan at a lower rate and using the proceeds to pay off your current loan. This makes sense when:

- Your credit score has improved since you originally borrowed.
- Market rates have dropped meaningfully.
- You've built a stronger income or lower debt-to-income ratio.

Before refinancing, calculate whether the savings outweigh any origination fees on the new loan. A lender charging a 2% origination fee on a small loan balance might eat into your interest savings.

### 2. Use Autopay Discounts

Many personal loan lenders offer a 0.25% to 0.50% rate discount if you enroll in automatic payments. It's a small but free reduction that most borrowers should take advantage of.

### What Won't Work

Calling your lender to negotiate a lower rate on a fixed personal loan rarely succeeds. Unlike credit cards, where issuers sometimes reduce APRs to retain customers, personal loans are typically securitized or sold on the secondary market. The original lender often can't modify terms even if they wanted to.

If your goal is the lowest possible rate on your next personal loan, focus on improving your credit score before applying. Even a 20-point increase can shift you into a better pricing tier. Check out strategies for building credit fast or review your credit report for errors that might be dragging your score down.

sections.heading: How to Get the Best Personal Loan Rate
sections.content:
Getting the lowest rate you qualify for isn't about luck. It's about preparation and comparison shopping.

**Step 1: Know your credit score.** Check it for free through your bank, credit card issuer, or a credit monitoring service. Understanding where you stand tells you which tier of rates to expect.

**Step 2: Prequalify with multiple lenders.** Most online lenders and credit unions let you check your rate with a soft inquiry. The CFPB encourages consumers to get multiple quotes, and rate shopping within a 14-day window typically counts as a single hard inquiry for scoring purposes.

**Step 3: Compare APRs, not just rates.** A loan with a lower interest rate but a hefty origination fee might cost more overall than a slightly higher rate with no fees.

**Step 4: Consider your loan term.** Shorter terms mean higher monthly payments but lower total interest. Run the math on both a 36-month and 60-month term to see the tradeoff.

**Step 5: Time your application.** If the Fed has signaled upcoming rate cuts, waiting a few months might get you a better rate. But don't wait indefinitely — the cost of carrying high-interest debt in the meantime might exceed any future rate savings.

For a side-by-side comparison of lenders offering competitive rates across credit tiers, CreditDoc's guide to the best personal loan lenders breaks down current options by credit score, loan amount, and fees.

faq_schema.question: What are credit union personal loan rates?
faq_schema.answer: Credit unions typically offer personal loan rates 1 to 3 percentage points below banks and online lenders, according to NCUA data. Their nonprofit structure allows them to pass savings to members through lower APRs and fewer fees.

faq_schema.question: Are personal loan interest rates going down?
faq_schema.answer: Personal loan rates follow the Federal Reserve's monetary policy direction. When the Fed cuts the federal funds rate, personal loan rates tend to decrease over the following weeks to months. Check the Fed's latest projections for forward guidance on rate trends.

faq_schema.question: Can I change my personal loan interest rate?
faq_schema.answer: You can't renegotiate a fixed-rate personal loan directly, but you can refinance into a new loan at a lower rate if your credit has improved or market rates have dropped. Many lenders also offer a 0.25%–0.50% autopay discount.

faq_schema.question: Do personal loans have higher interest rates than other loans?
faq_schema.answer: Yes, because most personal loans are unsecured. Without collateral, lenders charge more to offset their risk. However, personal loan rates are typically much lower than credit card rates, making them a popular choice for debt consolidation.

faq_schema.question: Does my personal loan rate change with the repo rate?
faq_schema.answer: The repo rate has only an indirect effect on personal loan pricing. The federal funds rate is the primary benchmark that influences consumer lending rates. Changes in the repo rate reflect interbank liquidity conditions rather than directly setting consumer loan terms.
